extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly identifies the protection of property owners as a core functional rule/duty of a king in a royal government.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all individuals wealthy enough to own landed estates.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve as royal bodyguard
proof: [§128] ...and a king’s body-guard consists of citizens, a tyrant’s of foreign mercenaries.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes that under a legitimate kingship, the protection of the monarch is a right or duty reserved for the citizens, whereas tyrants exclude citizens in favor of foreigners.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the general body of citizens in a monarchy.

proof: [§128] ...and in putting no trust in the multitude (which is why they resort to the measure of stripping the people of arms, and why ill-treatment of the mob and its expulsion from the city and settlement in scattered places is common to both forms of government, both oligarchy and tyranny)...
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies the systematic stripping of arms from the common people as a rule of thumb for both oligarchies and tyrannies to maintain control.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects the vast majority of the population (the 'multitude').

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement and urban residence
proof: [§128] ...and why ill-treatment of the mob and its expulsion from the city and settlement in scattered places is common to both forms of government, both oligarchy and tyranny...
extracted_rules.reasoning: Tyrannies and oligarchies utilize a rule of forced relocation/dispersal to prevent the common people from organizing in urban centers.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets the common mass of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from arbitrary execution or banishment
proof: [§128] ...while it copies democracy in making war on the notables and destroying them secretly and openly and banishing them as plotting against it and obstructive to its rule. Hence comes the advice of Periander to Thrasybulus, his docking of the prominent cornstalks, meaning that the prominent citizens must always be made away with.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Under tyranny, prominent or 'notable' citizens are systematically targeted for removal (death or exile) to eliminate competition for power.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This specifically targets the most 'prominent' or influential individuals in society.

proof: [§129] And also many men when enraged by the indignity of corporal chastisement have avenged the insult by destroying or attempting to destroy its author, even when a magistrate or member of a royal dynasty.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text treats corporal punishment as a 'shameful personal indignity' and an 'insult' that provokes revolution, appearing to imply that free/noble men in these societies were customarily exempt from such treatment by rulers.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies generally to free individuals of some standing, though the text focuses on 'notables'.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to common meals and club-fellowship
proof: [§132] these are both the measures mentioned some time back to secure the safety of a tyranny as far as possible—the lopping off of outstanding men and the destruction of the proud,—and also the prohibition of common meals and club-fellowship and education and all other things of this nature
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a standard practice in Greek tyrannies where the ruler prohibits social gatherings (common meals/clubs) to prevent the formation of mutual confidence and political plots.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies broadly to all subjects living under a tyrannical regime to prevent collective action.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to form study-circles and debate
proof: [§133] the prevention of the formation of study-circles and other conferences for debate, and the employment of every means that will make people as much as possible unknown to one another
extracted_rules.reasoning: Tyrants actively block intellectual and deliberative gatherings as a matter of policy to keep the population fragmented and uneducated.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to the general population, it specifically targets the educated or those inclined toward political debate.

proof: [§133] and to try not to be uninformed about any chance utterances or actions of any of the subjects, but to have spies like the women called provocatrices at Syracuse and the sharp-ears that used to be sent out by Hiero wherever there was any gathering or conference
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text details the systematic use of spies (provocatrices/sharp-ears) to monitor the speech and actions of all subjects under a tyranny.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This surveillance is a general condition of life for all subjects in the city.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain property and substance
proof: [§133] for in the reign of Dionysius the result of taxation used to be that in five years men had contributed the whole of their substance.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle cites historical examples of tyrants using excessive taxation to deliberately impoverish subjects, ensuring they lack the leisure to plot against the ruler.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects property owners and those with enough wealth to be taxed into poverty.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to leisure and personal labor
proof: [§133] Instances of this are the pyramids in Egypt and the votive offerings of the Cypselids, and the building of the temple of Olympian Zeus by the Pisistratidae and of the temples at Samos, works of Polycrates (for all these undertakings produce the same effect, constant occupation and poverty among the subject people)
extracted_rules.reasoning: Tyrants use large-scale building projects to keep the population constantly occupied and economically drained, preventing political engagement.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Such projects required the labor or financing of the massive majority of the subject population.

proof: [§133] dominance of women in the homes, in order that they may carry abroad reports against the men, and lack of discipline among the slaves, for the same reason
extracted_rules.reasoning: In tyrannical systems, slaves are encouraged to report on the activities of their masters, effectively gaining a form of state-sanctioned influence/protection through their role as informers.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to slaves living within households of the citizen class.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be companions of the ruler
proof: [§134] And it is a mark of a tyrant to have men of foreign extraction rather than citizens as guests at table and companions, feeling that citizens are hostile but strangers make no claim against him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Tyrants systematically exclude citizens from their inner circle and dining tables in favor of foreigners to reduce the risk of domestic plots.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This specifically affects the social and political elite among the citizens who would normally expect such access.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from corporal punishment and sexual outrage
proof: [§135] not only must he himself be known not to outrage any of his subjects, either boy or girl, but so also must everybody about him
[§136] And again he should carefully avoid all forms of outrage, and two beyond all, violent bodily punishments and outrage of the young.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle advises that for a tyranny to last, the ruler must adopt a 'regal' rule of protecting subjects from the types of bodily violence and sexual outrages that characterize failing regimes.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general protection that is supposed to extend to all subjects of the state.

proof: [§136] And he must pay such honor to those who display merit in any matter that they may think that they could never be more honored by the citizens if they were independent
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle advocates for a system where the ruler personally bestows honors upon those who show merit, creating a direct link of patronage.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Only the subset of the population demonstrating high merit is eligible for these honors.

extracted_rules.resource: Safety and protection from the opposite class
proof: [§136] the most important thing is for both to think that they owe their safety to the government and for it to prevent either from being wronged by the other
extracted_rules.reasoning: A key rule for the preservation of a stable tyranny/monarchy is the legal role of the government as a mediator that protects the rich from the poor and vice-versa.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This covers the two primary economic classes that constitute the entirety of the state.

proof: [§137] And they say that Pisistratus also once submitted to a summons for trial before the Areopagus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle cites the submission of the tyrant Pisistratus to the Areopagus court as a historical example of a ruler following the legal norms of the city rather than remaining above the law, which contributed to the stability of his rule.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to the highest individual office-holder (the tyrant/monarch) within the state's legal hierarchy.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to engage in commercial business
proof: [§139] and in many oligarchies those in office are not allowed to engage in business, but there are laws preventing it
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly mentions that certain oligarchies had laws prohibiting those holding political office from engaging in money-making or business activities to prevent corruption or distraction from public duty.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of the elite—those currently holding magistracies in an oligarchy.

proof: [§139] owners of much more than the average amount of property think it unjust for those who do not own any property to have an equal share in the state with those who do
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes an enforced norm in oligarchies where lack of property results in being denied an equal share in the state (voting or holding office), as the wealthy claim a greater right to power based on their assets.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a large segment of the population (the non-property owners) in any given oligarchical society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to engage in commercial business
proof: [§139] whereas in Carthage, which has a democratic government, the magistrates go in for business, and they have not yet had a revolution.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle contrasts Carthage with certain oligarchies, noting that Carthaginian law/custom permits those in high office to simultaneously participate in trade and business.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to the specific class of high officials within the Carthaginian state structure.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold honors and offices
proof: [§139] Furthermore men also form factions and cause revolutions in the constitution if they are not allowed a share of honors, and if they are unjustly or insolently treated
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies the systematic exclusion of certain groups from 'honors' (political status and offices) as a standard cause of social strife, implying that these groups are legally or customarily barred from such resources.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to various sub-groups or classes within a citizenry who are denied the status typically afforded to the ruling class.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold office regardless of wealth
proof: [§140] ...the following institutions are democratic in character: ... no property-qualification for office, or only a very low one;
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies the removal of wealth-based barriers to office as a core tenet of democratic systems, ensuring all citizens can participate in governance.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies broadly across the entire citizen body of many democratic poleis.

proof: [§140] But one factor of liberty is to govern and be governed in turn; ... the following institutions are democratic in character: ... no office to be held twice, or more than a few times, by the same person, or few offices except the military ones; short tenure either of all offices or of as many as possible;
extracted_rules.reasoning: In democracies, the principle of 'liberty' dictates that the right to rule must circulate among all citizens rather than being held permanently by an elite.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general constitutional rule affecting all citizens in democratic states.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve as a juror
proof: [§140] ...judicial functions to be exercised by all citizens, that is by persons selected from all, and on all matters, or on most and the greatest and most important,
extracted_rules.reasoning: A key democratic feature is the empowerment of the entire citizen body to act as the ultimate judicial authority.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to all citizens, it specifically concerns the legal/judiciary branch.

proof: [§140] Moreover the characteristics of a tyranny also are all thought to be democratic, I mean for instance licence among slaves, which may really be advantageous for the popular party up to a point, and among women and children, and indulgence to live as one likes;
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes that in extreme democracies, there is often a customary lack of strict control over the behavior/lifestyle of slaves, which he compares to tyranny.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to a specific social context (extreme democracy) and a specific legal status.

proof: [§140] Some of these offices are obviously not of a popular character, for instance that of Superintendent of Women and of Children; for the poor having no slaves are forced to employ their women and children as servants.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In poorer households, women lack the resource of leisure/exemption from menial labor because the family cannot afford slaves, forcing them into servant roles.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a subset of women (those in poor families) within the society.

proof: [§140] Hence those who are caring for the safety of the constitution must counteract this by enacting that nothing belonging to persons condemned at law shall be confiscated and liable to be carried to the public treasury, but that their property shall be consecrated to the service of religion;
extracted_rules.reasoning: To prevent demagogues from using courts for profit, some states redirected the property of the condemned to temples rather than the treasury, protecting it from being seized by the 'mob'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to individuals who have been formally condemned in a court of law.

extracted_rules.resource: Exclusive right to higher political/military office
proof: [§140] ...where the country happens to be suitable for horsemanship, there natural conditions favor the establishment of an oligarchy that will be powerful (for the security of the inhabitants depends on the strength of this element, and keeping studs of horses is the pursuit of those who own extensive estates); and where the ground is suitable for heavy infantry, conditions favor the next form of oligarchy (for heavy infantry is a service for the well-to-do rather than the poor);
extracted_rules.reasoning: In oligarchies, political power is linked to the ability to afford expensive military equipment (horses/heavy armor), granting the wealthy exclusive access to high-status military/political roles.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the wealthy elite within an oligarchic social structure.

proof: [§140] ...prohibiting the ownership of more than a certain amount of land under any conditions or else of more than a certain amount lying between a certain place and the citadel or city (and in early times at all events in many states there was even legislation prohibiting the sale of the original allotments;)
extracted_rules.reasoning: To maintain an agricultural democracy and prevent the concentration of power, laws were enacted to limit how much land a single citizen could own or sell.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all individuals capable of buying/selling land, specifically aiming to restrict the wealthy.

proof: [§140] ...admit to citizenship not only the legitimate children of citizens but also the base-born and those of citizen-birth on one side, I mean those whose father or mother is a citizen; for all this element is specially congenial to a to democracy of this sort.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Extreme democracies expand their power base by granting citizenship to those who would be excluded in more restrictive regimes due toleur lineage (e.g., only one citizen parent).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Specifically targets individuals of mixed parentage or illegitimate birth.

proof: [§140] ...where there are revenues, few meetings of the assembly must be held... so that the well-off do not take the pay and only the poor do... also payment for public duties, preferably in all branches, assembly, law-courts, magistracies,
extracted_rules.reasoning: Democracies often provide state pay for attending assemblies or courts, effectively acting as a redistribution of resources to ensure the poor can afford to participate in politics.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the large class of the 'poor' in democratic states.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to govern and be governed in turn
proof: [§143] But one factor of liberty is to govern and be governed in turn; for the popular principle of justice is to have equality according to number, not worth, and if this is the principle of justice prevailing, the multitude must of necessity be sovereign and the decision of the majority must be final and must constitute justice, for they say that each of the citizens ought to have an equal share.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies the reciprocal right to hold office and be subject to the law as a defining characteristic of democratic citizenship, based on numerical equality.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire body of citizens within a democratic state, representing a broad segment of the population.

proof: [§143] judicial functions to be exercised by all citizens, that is by persons selected from all, and on all matters, or on most and the greatest and most important, for instance the audit of official accounts, constitutional questions, private contracts;
extracted_rules.reasoning: In a democracy, the right to sit in judgment on legal and constitutional matters is extended to the whole citizen body.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This encompasses all individuals with citizen status in the described political system.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold the same office multiple times
proof: [§143] no office to be held twice, or more than a few times, by the same person, or few offices except the military ones; short tenure either of all offices or of as many as possible;
extracted_rules.reasoning: Democratic rules often prevent individuals from amassing power by strictly limiting the number of times an office can be held and the duration of the term.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While it applies to all citizens, it specifically limits those who have already attained office.

proof: [§145] and is a customary institution in it, for all the citizens to elect the magistrates and call them to account, and to try law-suits, but for the holders of the greatest magistracies to be elected and to have property-qualifications, the higher offices being elected from the higher property-grades
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a specific type of 'best' democracy where, despite general voting rights, the most powerful offices are reserved for those meeting high property requirements.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule specifically targets the segment of the citizenry that does not meet the wealth threshold for high office.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to payment for public duties
proof: [§143] also payment for public duties, preferably in all branches, assembly, law-courts, magistracies, or if not, for the magistracies, the law-courts, council and sovereign assemblies
extracted_rules.reasoning: A key democratic feature is providing wages for civic participation, ensuring that those without private wealth can afford to exercise their political rights.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all citizens who participate in the specified civic functions.

proof: [§143] the assembly to be sovereign over all matters, but no official over any or only over extremely few;
extracted_rules.reasoning: In democratic systems, the collective body of citizens (the assembly) is granted supreme authority over state matters, limiting the power of individual officials.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to the collective rights of the citizen body as a whole.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to audit and account magistrates
proof: [§145] to have control over electing magistrates and calling them to account makes up for the lack of office... for all the citizens to elect the magistrates and call them to account, and to try law-suits
extracted_rules.reasoning: Even in democracies where the poor might not hold high office, they retain the legal right to audit and prosecute officials for their conduct in office.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general right afforded to the multitude within the citizen body.

proof: [§146] For the purpose of making the people an agricultural community, not only were some of the laws that were enacted in many states in early times entirely serviceable, prohibiting the ownership of more than a certain amount of land under any conditions or else of more than a certain amount lying between a certain place and the citadel or city
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes historical and still-relevant laws that capped the amount of land a citizen could own to maintain a specific social and agricultural order.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all land-owning citizens but is specific to certain geographic zones or total acreage limits.

proof: [§146] (and in early times at all events in many states there was even legislation prohibiting the sale of the original allotments; and there is a law said to be due to Oxylus with some similar provision, forbidding loans secured on a certain portion of a man’s existing estate)
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites specific legislation that prevented citizens from selling their primary land allotments or using them as collateral, restricting their right to alienate property.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies specifically to holders of 'original allotments', a subset of the land-owning class.

proof: [§147] With a view to setting up this kind of democracy and making the people powerful their leaders usually acquire as many supporters as possible and admit to citizenship not only the legitimate children of citizens but also the base-born and those of citizen-birth on one side, I mean those whose father or mother is a citizen
extracted_rules.reasoning: In extreme democracies, the rules of citizenship are expanded to include those previously excluded due to lineage (illegitimacy) or having only one citizen parent.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects a significant demographic group within the city-state population regarding their legal status.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to maintain traditional kinship groups
proof: [§147] A democracy of this kind will also find useful such institutions as were employed by Cleisthenes at Athens... different tribes and brotherhoods must be created outnumbering the old ones, and the celebrations of private religious rites must be grouped together into a small number of public celebrations, and every device must be employed to make all the people as much as possible intermingled with one another, and to break up the previously existing groups of associates.
extracted_rules.reasoning: State-mandated restructuring of tribes and religious rites forced citizens to abandon traditional local/familial associations in favor of new, state-mixed groups.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a wholesale reorganization of the entire citizen body's social and political structure.

proof: [§148] Hence those who are caring for the safety of the constitution must counteract this by enacting that nothing belonging to persons condemned at law shall be confiscated and liable to be carried to the public treasury, but that their property shall be consecrated to the service of religion
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle advocates for a rule where the property of the wealthy (notables) is protected from state seizure upon conviction, redirecting it to religious use to discourage malicious prosecution by the poor.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies specifically to the wealthy who are targeted in law-courts.

proof: [§149] also they have divided the whole number of their magistracies into two classes, one elected by vote and the other filled by lot,—the latter to ensure that the people may have a share in them
extracted_rules.reasoning: The use of the lot (sortition) is a specific mechanism used in Tarentum and elsewhere to ensure the poor/common people can access political offices they might lose in a formal vote.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Relates to the general populace's ability to enter the governing class.

proof: [§149] and for it the property-qualifications must be divided into one group of smaller properties and another of larger ones, smaller properties qualifying their owners for the indispensable offices and larger ones for the more important
extracted_rules.reasoning: In oligarchic systems, political power is explicitly tiered based on wealth levels, with the poorest excluded from high-level offices.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Divides the entire citizen population into ranks based on wealth assessments.

proof: [§149] while in the meantime the well-to-do must contribute pay for attendance at the necessary assemblies, being themselves excused from useless public services.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text suggests a trade-off where the wealthy provide funds for the poor's attendance in exchange for being exempt from other burdensome civic duties (liturgies).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies specifically to the 'well-to-do' demographic.

proof: [§150] And the bestowal of a share in the government upon the multitude should either go on the lines stated before, and be made to those who acquire the property-qualification, or as at Thebes, to people after they have abstained for a time from mechanic industries
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the property qualification as a method to determine who receives a 'share in the government,' effectively excluding those below a certain wealth threshold from participating in the constitution.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the 'multitude' or the lower-wealth strata of the citizen body, which often constitutes a large portion of the population.

proof: [§150] ...or as at Thebes, to people after they have abstained for a time from mechanic industries
extracted_rules.reasoning: In certain Greek states like Thebes, engaging in manual or 'mechanic' labor served as a legal bar to political participation until a period of abstinence from such work had passed.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It targets a specific economic class (artisans/mechanics) within the citizen body.

proof: [§150] And furthermore the most supreme offices also, which must be retained by those within the constitution, must have expensive duties attached to them, in order that the common people may be willing to be excluded from them, and may feel no resentment against the ruling class, because it pays a high price for office.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a structural rule where high offices are attached to 'expensive duties' (liturgies), which functions as a material barrier ensuring only the wealthy hold these positions while the poor 'willingly' accept exclusion due to the cost.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the 'common people' (the multitude) regarding the highest tiers of government.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve in the cavalry

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This concerns the division of the entire citizen body into military classes based on wealth.

proof: [§152] Hence there must not be one magistracy specially assigned to the custody of prisoners nor must the same magistracy perform this duty continuously, but it should be performed by the young, in places where there is a regiment of cadets or guards, and by the magistrates, in successive sections.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Because the custody of prisoners is 'irksome' and avoided by 'respectable people,' the duty is specifically shifted onto the young (cadets/guards) to distribute the 'odium' of the office.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to those of 'military age' or 'cadets' (ephebes) in certain types of states.

proof: [§153] On the other hand, peculiar to the states that have more leisure and prosperity... are the offices of Superintendent of Women, Guardian of the Laws, Superintendent of Children

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to half of the population (women) in wealthy/aristocratic states.

proof: [§155] For the artisan class has no share in the state, nor has any other class that is not an artificer of virtue.

extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly excludes the 'mechanic' (artisan) class from citizenship and a 'share in the state' in his ideal constitution because their lifestyle is supposedly'ignoble' and prevents the development of virtue necessary for political participation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a broad socio-economic class of laborers and skilled workers common in Greek city-states.

proof: [§155] ...nor yet must those who are to be citizens in the best state be tillers of the soil (for leisure is needed both for the development of virtue and for active participation in politics).

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Agricultural laborers formed a significant portion of ancient populations.

proof: [§155] Nature has given the distinction by making the group that is itself the same in race partly younger and partly older, of which two sets it is appropriate to the one to be governed and for the other to govern; and no one chafes or thinks himself better than his rulers when he is governed on the ground of age, especially as he is going to get back what he has thus contributed to the common stock when he reaches the proper age.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule where the right to govern is reserved for elders, while the young are restricted to being governed until they reach the appropriate age.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all male citizens of a certain age bracket within the society.

proof: [§155] ...since it is seemly that those who have relinquished these duties owing to age should render to the gods their due worship and should spend their retirement in their service, it is to these that the priestly offices should be assigned.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of the elderly citizen population.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a spatial restriction affecting specific occupational groups.

proof: [§155] Therefore it is fitting for the women to be married at about the age of eighteen and the men at thirty-seven or a little before...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The lawgiver sets specific age minimums for marriage to ensure biological compatibility and health, restricting the freedom of younger individuals to marry.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all females reaching the age of maturity.

proof: [§155] On the other hand it is not necessary for states to include the teeming population that grows up in connection with common sailors, as there is no need for these to be citizens...
extracted_rules.reasoning: Common sailors are explicitly excluded from the right to citizenship in the best state, distinguishing them from the marines who are free men and part of the infantry.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to a specific vocational class within the maritime military/economy.

proof: [§155] First therefore we must consider whether some regulation in regard to the boys ought to be instituted, next whether it is advantageous for their supervision to be conducted on a public footing...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text argues for the state-mandated and publicly funded/managed education and supervision of male children of the citizen class.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the entire juvenile portion of the citizen class.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to wear a belt instead of a halter
proof: [§159] and at one time there was also a law in Macedonia that a man who had never killed an enemy must wear his halter instead of a belt.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific Macedonian law where the lack of a military kill resulted in a visible loss of a standard dress item (the belt), replaced by a humiliating halter, effectively denying the individual a mark of adult male status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a subset of the male population (those who haven't killed an enemy) within a specific kingdom.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in communal ritual drinking
proof: [§159] Among Scythian tribes at a certain festival a cup was carried round from which a man that had not killed an enemy was not allowed to drink.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The custom explicitly bars men from a specific shared ritual resource based on their lack of military achievement.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to certain individuals in Scythian tribes who fail to meet a specific military requirement.

proof: [§159] for instance at Carthage, we are told, warriors receive the decoration of armlets of the same number as the campaigns on which they have served
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law grants warriors concrete physical honors/decorations based on their length of service, which constitutes a gain in social/material standing.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is restricted specifically to warriors who have completed campaigns in Carthage.

proof: [§159] Yet it is strange if there is not a natural distinction between peoples suited to be despotically ruled and those not suited; so that if this is so, it is not proper to attempt to exercise despotic government over all people, but only over those suited for it
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle validates the practice of 'despotic rule' (mastery) over specific groups he deems 'suited for it' by nature, thereby excluding them from the right to be ruled as free citizens.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This concerns a very large segment of the population (those designated as slaves by nature).

proof: [§160] there is nothing specially dignified in employing a slave, as a slave, for giving orders about menial duties has in it nothing of nobility
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the role of the slave specifically with 'menial duties,' confirming that slaves lack the right to refuse degrading or non-noble labor.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire class of slaves.

proof: [§158] which mode of life is the more desirable, the life of active citizenship and participation in politics, or rather the life of an alien and that of detachment from the political partnership
extracted_rules.reasoning: The distinction between the citizen and the 'alien' (resident alien/metic) is defined by the alien's 'detachment from the political partnership,' meaning they are excluded from participating in the governance of the state.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all non-citizen residents within the Greek city-states.

proof: [§161] For a man’s acts can no longer be noble if he does not excel as greatly as a man excels a woman or a father his children or a master his slaves, so that one who transgresses cannot afterwards achieve anything sufficient to rectify the lapse from virtue that he had already committed.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle frames the excellence of a man as being naturally defined by his superiority over women, children, and slaves. This reflects a social rule where men are granted a higher tier of 'nobility' and authority within the social and domestic hierarchy.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the most basic units of society (households) and affects the majority of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be considered a part of the state
proof: [§162] All the same, even if it be right to judge the state by the test of its multitude, this ought not to be done with regard to the multitude of any and every class (for states are doubtless bound to contain a large number of slaves and resident aliens and foreigners), but the test should be the number of those who are a part of the state—the special parts of which a state consists.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly distinguishes between those who simply reside in a territory (slaves, aliens) and those who are 'parts of the state,' effectively excluding the former from the civic body and its associated privileges.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to large demographic blocks within Greek city-states that were essential to the economy but excluded from power.

extracted_rules.resource: Distribution of offices according to merit
proof: [§163] but in order to decide questions of justice and in order to distribute the offices according to merit it is necessary for the citizens to know each other’s personal characters, since where this does not happen to be the case the business of electing officials and trying law-suits is bound to go badly.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule of political distribution where offices are meant to be granted based on merit and known character, rather than at random, which is a resource for citizens of high repute.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to citizens, the emphasis on merit and personal knowledge naturally narrows the pool of likely office-holders.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from detection for usurping rights
proof: [§163] Also in such a community it is easy for foreigners and resident aliens to usurp the rights of citizenship, for the excessive number of the population makes it not difficult to escape detection.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle suggests that in a well-ordered state (one that is 'taken in at one view'), resident aliens are prevented from 'usurping' citizenship, indicating a strict legal barrier to their entry into the citizen class.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects the significant population of 'metoikoi' (resident aliens) in Greek cities.

proof: [§164] it is manifest that if any advantage does result through the communication of city with port the state will possess this advantage, and if there is any harmful result it is easy to guard against it by means of laws stating and regulating what persons are not and what persons are to have intercourse with one another.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the use of law to restrict who may interact with the citizen population, specifically aimed at traders and foreigners to prevent the corruption of 'law and order.'
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule specifically targets the merchant/trader class and visiting foreigners in maritime/port contexts.

proof: [§165] On the other hand it is not necessary for states to include the teeming population that grows up in connection with common sailors, as there is no need for these to be citizens; for the marines are free men and are a part of the infantry, and it is they who have command and control the crew.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle distinguishes between the 'marines' (who are free and in command) and the 'common sailors' or 'crew', explicitly stating there is no need for the latter to be granted the status of citizens.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific occupational class within maritime states.

proof: [§168] For the artisan class has no share in the state, nor has any other class that is not an artificer of virtue.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly excludes the 'artisan class' (technitai) from having a 'share in the state' (citizenship/political participation) because their occupation is seen as incompatible with the development of virtue.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This covers a broad segment of the urban working population.

proof: [§167] ...it is therefore clear from these considerations that in the most nobly constituted state, and the one that possesses men that are absolutely just, not merely just relatively to the principle that is the basis of the constitution, the citizens must not live a mechanic or a mercantile life (for such a life is ignoble and inimical to virtue)...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text bars those engaged in trade or 'mercantile life' from citizenship in the ideal state because such lives are deemed 'ignoble'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This excludes the entire commercial class from political life.

proof: [§167] ...nor yet must those who are to be citizens in the best state be tillers of the soil (for leisure is needed both for the development of virtue and for active participation in politics).
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle excludes farmers ('tillers of the soil') from citizenship because the physical demands of their labor prevent the 'leisure' required for political activity.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the primary laboring class of the ancient world.

proof: [§168] Moreover the ownership of properties also must be centered round these classes, for the citizens must necessarily possess plentiful means, and these are the citizens. [...] And it is also manifest that the properties must belong to these classes, inasmuch as it is necessary for the tillers of the soil to be slaves, or serfs of alien race.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that property should be reserved for the citizen classes (military and deliberative), while those who actually work the land (whom he identifies as slaves or serfs) are excluded from ownership.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This defines a major structural divide between the propertied and unpropertied classes.

proof: [§168] ...as in the natural order of things strength is found in the younger men and wisdom in the elder, it seems to be expedient and just for their functions to be allotted to both in this way, for this mode of division possesses conformity with merit.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule where political/deliberative power is reserved for those in the 'elder' prime of life, while younger men are restricted to military service.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all citizens as they age.

proof: [§168] Priests must be appointed neither from the tillers of the soil nor from the artisans, for it is seemly that the gods should be worshipped by citizens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly bars farmers and artisans from priestly offices, reserving these roles strictly for citizens.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Relates to a specific set of prestigious offices.

proof: [§168] ...since the citizen body is divided into two parts, the military class and the councillor class, and as it is seemly that those who have relinquished these duties owing to age should render to the gods their due worship and should spend their retirement in their service, it is to these that the priestly offices should be assigned.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The society assigns priestly roles specifically to citizens who have aged out of military and political duties, effectively creating a retirement system based on religious office.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Targets a specific age-demarcated subgroup of the citizen body.

proof: [§169] It has been stated before that the land ought to be owned by those who possess arms and those who share the rights of the constitution, and why the cultivators ought to be a different caste from these, and what is the proper extent and conformation of the country.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle reaffirms the Greek political principle that land ownership is a prerequisite and a right reserved for those with full civic status (citizens/soldiers), explicitly separating them from the laboring class.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule defines the primary property-holding class of the entire city-state.

proof: [§169] It has been stated before that the land ought to be owned by those who possess arms and those who share the rights of the constitution, and why the cultivators ought to be a different caste from these
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a caste-based separation where the people actually working the land (cultivators) are legally barred from owning it.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the entire laboring class involved in agriculture.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in war deliberations
proof: [§170] Hence some people have a law that the citizens whose land is near the frontier are not to take part in deliberation as to wars against neighboring states, on the ground that private interest would prevent them from being able to take counsel wisely.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle cites a specific legal practice where a sub-group of citizens is stripped of their right to vote or speak on specific foreign policy matters due to their geographic/economic vulnerability.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies only to a specific geographic subset of the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to attain freedom through merit
proof: [§170] How slaves should be employed, and why it is advantageous that all slaves should have their freedom set before them as a reward, we will say later.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes a customary/legal practice of offering freedom as a formal reward, providing a concrete mechanism for changing status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the general slave population.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement to central political sites
proof: [§172] It is convenient that below this site should be laid out an agora of the kind customary in Thessaly which they call a free agora, that is, one which has to be kept clear of all merchandise and into which no artisan or farmer or any other such person may intrude unless summoned by the magistrates.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The 'free agora' is a restricted civic space where manual laborers are legally prohibited from entering, preserving the space for the leisure of the ruling class.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Targets specific occupational groups within the free population.

proof: [§170] we hold that no citizen should be ill supplied with means of subsistence. ... Of the common land one portion should be assigned to the services of religion, and the other to defray the cost of the common meals
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law provides for public land to fund common meals, ensuring that even poor citizens have access to food and maintain their standing.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all members of the citizen body.

proof: [§172] It would give amenity to the site if the gymnasia of the older men were also situated here—for it is proper to have this institution also divided according to ages
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a system where social and physical institutions (gymnasia) are strictly segregated by age, granting elders their own exclusive spaces near the seat of power.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the specific demographic of older male citizens.

proof: [§174] But then the virtue of the state is of course caused by the citizens who share in its government being virtuous; and in our state all the citizens share in the government.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle explicitly states that in the constitution he is describing as the 'best', the status of citizen carries a guaranteed right to participate in the governing process.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire class of citizens within the state, which is a broad and foundational group.

proof: [§175] it is clear that for many reasons it is necessary for all to share alike in ruling and being ruled in turn. For equality means for persons who are alike identity of status, and also it is difficult for a constitution to endure that is framed in contravention of justice.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that because citizens are fundamentally alike in status, justice requires that they all have the opportunity to hold office (rule) rather than being permanent subjects.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all individuals meeting the criteria of 'alike' status (citizens), but describes a specific mechanism of power distribution.

proof: [§175] Nature has given the distinction by making the group that is itself the same in race partly younger and partly older, of which two sets it is appropriate to the one to be governed and for the other to govern; and no one chafes or thinks himself better than his rulers when he is governed on the ground of age
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle uses age as a natural stratification axis, determining that younger citizens must be the subjects (ruled) while older citizens are the rulers, with the expectation of future reversal.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule narrows the broad citizen group down to a specific life-stage (the younger) who are temporarily denied the resource.

proof: [§176] Hence a number of what are thought to be menial services can be honorably performed even by freemen in youth; since in regard to honor and dishonor actions do not differ so much in themselves as in their end and object.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text indicates a customary rule where free citizens, during their youth, are expected or permitted to perform tasks otherwise considered menial or 'slavish' as part of their training to eventually rule.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This targets a intersectional group (young + free) and a specific type of social activity (menial service).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to leisure and virtuous activity
proof: [§178] Therefore courage and fortitude are needed for business, love of wisdom for leisure, temperance and justice for both seasons... since, as the proverb goes, there is no leisure for slaves
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle cites a cultural proverb to affirm a societal rule: the condition of slavery is defined by the total absence of leisure (schole), which is the prerequisite for the 'good life' and virtue.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general defining characteristic of the entire slave class in Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold despotic power over 'natural slaves'
proof: [§177] The proper object of practising military training is... thirdly to hold despotic power over those who deserve to be slaves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a concrete military and legal aim: the right of the citizen-soldier to conquer and rule (despotically) over specific groups designated by nature as 'deserving' of slavery.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This pertains to the specific military function of a state and its interaction with specific non-citizen groups.

proof: [§181] Therefore persons exceeding this age by four or five years must be discharged from the duty of producing children for the community, and for the rest of their lives if they have intercourse it must be manifestly for the sake of health or for some other similar reason.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a societal rule where men over the age of approximately 54-55 are legally released from their civic obligation to produce offspring for the state, shifting the purpose of intercourse to private health.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all male citizens within a specific elder age bracket.

proof: [§181] As to exposing or rearing the children born, let there be a law that no deformed child shall be reared.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mandates a law prohibiting the rearing of children born with physical deformities, effectively requiring their exposure/death.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a very specific sub-group of the population based on physical condition at birth.

proof: [§181] And pregnant women also must take care of their bodies, not avoiding exercise nor adopting a low diet; this it is easy for the lawgiver to secure by ordering them to make a journey daily for the due worship of the deities whose office is the control of childbirth.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The lawgiver enforces a specific physical regimen on pregnant women, using religious journeys as a mechanism to ensure they do not remain sedentary.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all women during the duration of pregnancy.

proof: [§181] As to intercourse with another woman or man... any who may be discovered doing anything of the sort during their period of parentage must be punished with a loss of privilege suited to the offence.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Infidelity during the years designated for raising a family results in a formal legal penalty involving the loss of civic privileges (atimia).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to any married citizen during their child-bearing years.

proof: [§183] But the younger ones must not be allowed in the audience at lampoons and at comedy, before they reach the age at which they will now have the right to recline at table in company and to drink deeply.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Legal restrictions prevent minors from being spectators at specific types of public performances (comedy/lampoons) until they reach the age of majority/symposia participation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all male children until they reach the age of 21 (or the local age of reclining at meals).

proof: [§183] anybody found saying or doing any of the things prohibited, if he is of free station but not yet promoted to reclining at the public meals, must be punished with marks of dishonor and with beating.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Free-born youths who have not yet reached adulthood (marked by the right to recline at public meals) are subject to physical beating for indecent speech or actions, a punishment adult citizens are generally protected from.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all free-born males of a specific age range.

proof: [§181] if the regular customs hinder any of those born being exposed, there must be a limit fixed to the procreation of offspring, and if any people have a child as a result of intercourse in contravention of these regulations, abortion must be practised.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The state imposes a hard limit on the number of children a family can have to prevent overpopulation, mandating abortion if that limit is exceeded.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general regulation affecting the reproductive rights of the entire citizen body.

proof: [§182] The Tutors must supervise the children’s pastimes, and in particular must see that they associate as little as possible with slaves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Institutional supervisors (Tutors) are mandated to limit the contact between free children and enslaved persons to prevent moral 'taint'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all free children under the age of seven.

proof: [§184] next whether it is advantageous for their supervision to be conducted on a public footing or in a private manner as is done at present in most states, and thirdly of what particular nature this supervision ought to be.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes that in most contemporary Greek states, the supervision and upbringing of boys is conducted in a 'private manner.' This indicates that children in these societies do not have a standard right to public oversight or state-funded educational infrastructure.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule describes a general state of affairs for the majority of male children in most Greek city-states of the author's time.

proof: [§185] And inasmuch as the end for the whole state is one, it is manifest that education also must necessarily be one and the same for all and that the superintendence of this must be public, and not on private lines, in the way in which at present each man superintends the education of his own children, teaching them privately, and whatever special branch of knowledge he thinks fit. But matters of public interest ought to be under public supervision; at the same time we ought not to think that any of the citizens belongs to himself, but that all belong to the state, for each is a part of the state, and it is natural for the superintendence of the several parts to have regard to the superintendence of the whole.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle argues that education should be a state-controlled, public resource rather than a private family matter, integrating children into the civic body.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies broadly to the children of all recognized citizens within the state.

proof: [§185] It is therefore not difficult to see that the young must be taught those useful arts that are indispensably necessary; but it is clear that they should not be taught all the useful arts, those pursuits that are liberal being kept distinct from those that are illiberal, and that they must participate in such among the useful arts as will not render the person who participates in them vulgar. A task and also an art or a science must be deemed vulgar if it renders the body or soul or mind of free men useless for the employments and actions of virtue.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule that free citizens should be protected from certain types of vocational training ('illiberal' or 'vulgar' arts) because such labor is considered to degrade the capacity for civic virtue.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the class of free citizens, distinguishing them from laborers and slaves.

extracted_rules.resource: Legal standing and social capacity for virtue
proof: [§185] Hence we entitle vulgar all such arts as deteriorate the condition of the body, and also the industries that earn wages; for they make the mind preoccupied and degraded.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly categorizes wage-earning as an activity that 'degrades' the mind, identifying this group as lacking the mental/moral state required for full civic participation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It targets a specific economic class—those who work for wages rather than living on stored wealth or land.

proof: [§185] And since we reject professional education in the instruments and in performance (and we count performance in competitions as professional, for the performer does not take part in it for his own improvement, but for his hearers’ pleasure, and that a vulgar pleasure, owing to which we do not consider performing to be proper for free men, but somewhat menial; and indeed performers do become vulgar, since the object at which they aim is a low one, as vulgarity in the audience usually influences the music...)
extracted_rules.reasoning: Professional musicianship is classified as 'menial' and 'vulgar,' thus free men are barred from pursuing it as a profession to maintain their status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This defines the boundaries of acceptable professional activity for the entire free citizen class.

proof: [§185] For until puberty we should apply lighter exercises, forbidding hard diet and severe exertions, in order that nothing may hinder the growth; for there is no small proof that too severe training can produce this result in the fact that in the list of Olympic victors one would only find two or three persons who have won both as men and as boys, because when people go into training in youth the severe exercises rob them of their strength.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The lawgiver is advised to protect children from extreme physical exertion, which is reserved for a later stage of life to ensure proper growth.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a specific rule applying to a narrow biological window (childhood to puberty).

proof: [§185] and since the audience is of two classes, one freemen and educated people, and the other the vulgar class composed of mechanics and laborers and other such persons, the latter sort also must be assigned competitions and shows for relaxation; and just as their souls are warped from the natural state, so those harmonies and melodies that are highly strung and irregular in coloration are deviations, but people of each sort receive pleasure from what is naturally suited to them
extracted_rules.reasoning: The state provides specific forms of entertainment (lower-quality or 'warped' music) specifically tailored to the working class for their relaxation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the specific segment of the population engaged in manual labor.

proof: [§185] for instance, those whose powers have waned through lapse of time cannot easily sing the highly strung harmonies, but to persons of that age nature suggests the relaxed harmonies. Hence even with a view to the period of life that is to follow, that of the comparatively old, it is proper to engage in the harmonies and melodies of this kind too
extracted_rules.reasoning: The society allows/provides specific 'relaxed' musical modes for the elderly whose physical capabilities have declined, ensuring they remain included in musical culture.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies specifically to the elderly population based on biological aging.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entirety of the citizen class within the political community.

proof: [§187] it is clear that they should not be taught all the useful arts, those pursuits that are liberal being kept distinct from those that are illiberal, and that they must participate in such among the useful arts as will not render the person who participates in them vulgar. extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all free-born youths undergoing the educational system.

proof: [§187] Hence we entitle vulgar all such arts as deteriorate the condition of the body, and also the industries that earn wages; for they make the mind preoccupied and degraded.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to a specific economic class (those working for wages) within the free population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to education in music and pastime
proof: [§188] for they rank it as a form of pastime that they think proper for free men. ... It is clear therefore that there is a form of education in which boys should be trained not because it is useful or necessary but as being liberal and noble
extracted_rules.reasoning: Music and noble pastimes are specifically designated as the right of free men, serving as the proper use of their leisure time.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the class of free citizens as a whole.

proof: [§189] For until puberty we should apply lighter exercises, forbidding hard diet and severe exertions, in order that nothing may hinder the growth; ... [§190] But when they have spent three years after puberty upon their other studies, then it is suitable to occupy the next period of life with laborious exercises and strict training diet
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines a specific age-based rule: children before puberty are legally/customarily protected from severe physical exertion to preserve their growth, with laborious training reserved for older youths.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general age-stratified rule for the developing citizen population.

extracted_rules.rule_name: Separation of mental and physical labor
extracted_rules.resource: Protection from simultaneous mental and physical toil
proof: [§190] for it is wrong to work hard with the mind and the body at the same time; for it is the nature of the two different sorts of exertion to produce opposite effects, bodily toil impeding the development of the mind and mental toil that of the body.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule for the organization of life/education, stating that the state must not force citizens to undergo heavy mental and physical labor simultaneously due to their conflicting natural effects.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to all citizens undergoing state training.

proof: [§191] professional musicians we speak of as vulgar people, and indeed we think it not manly to perform music, except when drunk or for fun.
[§195] we do not consider performing to be proper for free men, but somewhat menial; and indeed performers do become vulgar, since the object at which they aim is a low one
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes a societal norm in 4th-century Greece where professional musical performance is viewed as 'vulgar' and 'menial,' effectively excluding free citizens from pursuing it as a profession without losing their social/manly status.
